ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated stewed-tomato code; A00LC (tomatoes, cooked/processed) is the closest defensible match, representing a broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for stewed tomato as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stewed tomato is approximately 120 g (about half a cup), suitable as a side dish or topping.

nutrient_source.title: Stewed Tomatoes — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14040060 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for stewed tomato exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: canned tomatoes / cooked tomatoes, which are consistently reported in the low-GI range (GI 35–38) in peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 13.05 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 5.5 for a 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 455 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid stewed tomato prepared with added salt; sodium at 455 mg/100 g significantly exceeds the recommended threshold, and home preparation with reduced or no added salt is strongly advised to bring sodium below 400 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (7.5 g/100 g) offset by low GI (~35) and dietary fibre (1.7 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stewed tomato has a low glycemic index (~35) and provides 1.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g, which moderates postprandial glucose absorption; individuals with type 2 diabetes may include it in moderate portions (approximately 120 g) while monitoring total carbohydrate intake and avoiding additional sugar during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (455 mg/100 g) is a cardiovascular risk factor; fat content is low (2.68 g/100 g) and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low fat and zero cholesterol profile of stewed tomato is favorable for cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content (455 mg/100 g) poses a risk for blood pressure elevation and should be mitigated by choosing low-sodium preparations or reducing added salt during cooking.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (solanine content) and elevated sodium (455 mg/100 g) may exacerbate joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tomatoes are nightshade vegetables; solanine may trigger joint inflammation in susceptible individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, and the elevated sodium (455 mg/100 g) may worsen tissue swelling — individuals with RA should monitor personal tolerance and prefer low-sodium preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stewed tomato is a cooked vegetable preparation made from ripe red tomatoes that have been simmered or braised until soft and tender, typically with added salt, sugar, onion, celery, and green pepper. The result is a dish of soft, red tomato pieces with a tangy-sweet flavour and a tender, yielding texture. It is widely used as a side dish, a topping for proteins, or an ingredient in composite dishes. Per 100 g, stewed tomato provides 79 kcal, 1.96 g protein, 2.68 g fat, 13.05 g carbohydrate, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 455 mg sodium. The sodium content is notably elevated due to added salt during preparation, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis without sodium reduction. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), and the dietary fibre moderates glucose absorption, making it acceptable for type 2 diabetes in controlled portions. Tomatoes are a nightshade vegetable and may aggravate joint symptoms in sensitive rheumatoid arthritis patients. The food is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is a source of potassium (247 mg/100 g), calcium (26 mg/100 g), and iron (1.06 mg/100 g). Cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g. Data sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for stewed tomato (F14040060) as of 2026-04-16. 2. sugar_g value of 7.5 g/100 g is carried forward from the input health_suitability reasoning text, which references this figure; it is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for canned stewed tomatoes (USDA SR Legacy item 11530, ~6.9–7.5 g sugar/100 g). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; stewed tomato is a plant-based food and inherently contains no cholesterol. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on canned/cooked tomatoes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008); no direct stewed-tomato GI entry exists in the approved GI database. 5. FoodEx2 code A00LC is an approximate match; no dedicated stewed-tomato code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. 6. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a generic Western preparation. Multilingual naming requirements beyond English do not apply under the cuisine-based rules in PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 7.
